data.title: Toast: Focus On ARR Growth And EBITDA Expansion
data.description:
As we wrap up the Q4 earnings season, it's becoming increasingly difficult to avoid the selling pressure that is overtaking the stock market. Despite healthy results from most companies, investors have sold off stocks (particularly growth stocks) on a combination of macro and geopolitical fears.

Toast (TOST), the restaurant software platform and payments processor, has been sharply hit this year. Since January, the stock has lost ~20% of its value, and relative to 2025 highs just shy of $50, the stock has suffered a ~40% decline. The question for investors now is, can Toast stage a turnaround?

I last wrote a buy article on Toast in December, when the stock was trading at $37 per share. Since then, Toast has shed a third of its market value. While it's true that the company is facing secular headwinds from restaurant sector challenges, sales execution has been tremendously strong, with the company noting market share gains and showcasing strong payment volumes. I'm reiterating my buy rating here.

Amid the intense selling pressure in this stock over the past year, it's a good opportunity for investors to reassess the bull case in this name. To me, the core drivers of the bull thesis for Toast are:

  • Expanding beyond payments and into an operational software platform for restaurants. The company's rising ARR is a signal that more and more restaurants are bundling in more comprehensive software solutions. Subscription revenue in FY25 grew at a 33% y/y pace.
  • Amid compression in same-restaurant sales growth, Toast is achieving double-digit GPV growth. Outside of a few limited standouts like McDonald's (MCD), most restaurant companies, particularly in the fast-casual sector, have seen decelerating and declining same-restaurant sales growth. In spite of this, Toast is achieving double-digit growth in GPV (gross payments volumes) that suggests that the company's new business pickups are offsetting overall industry malaise.
  • Meaningful market share gains, but still plenty of room for expansion. The company notes that it has risen to covering 20% of total SMB and mid-market restaurants in the U.S. This gives Toast plenty of credibility, but continues to leave plenty of room for expansion.
  • Immense growth in profit margins and free cash flow. Toast doubled its FCF in FY25. The company's expanding flywheel of software subscriptions is driving economies of scale for the company.

We note that Toast's decline since the start of the year has led to meaningful compression in valuation multiples that has made the stock much more buyable. At current share prices near $27, Toast trades at a market cap of $16.14 billion. After we net off the $1.99 billion of cash on the company's latest balance sheet, Toast's resulting enterprise value is $14.15 billion.

For FY26, Toast is guiding to $775-$790 million in adjusted EBITDA, or 22-25% y/y growth.

This positions Toast's valuation at 18.1x EV/FY26 adjusted EBITDA. Considering the company's >20% adjusted EBITDA growth and >20% revenue/GPV growth, market share gains, and expanding sector coverage (powering not just restaurants, but retail and grocery stores as well), this represents an attractive buying opportunity in this stock.

Stay long here and buy the dip.

Q4 download

Let's now go through Toast's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q4 earnings summary is shown below:

Toast's revenue grew 22% y/y to $1.63 billion, beating Wall Street's expectations of $1.62 billion (+21% y/y) by a one-point margin. Revenue growth did decelerate from 25% y/y growth in Q3. Gross payment volumes of $51.4 billion still grew 22% y/y but decelerated versus 24% growth in Q3.

However, we have to take this deceleration in the context of a weak restaurant industry. Chipotle (CMG), a bellwether for the restaurant sector, suffered a -2.5% y/y decline in comp sales in Q4:

Toast, meanwhile, continues to gain share across not just restaurants but retail as well. The company added 8,000 net-new paid locations within the quarter. Per CEO Aman Narang's remarks on the Q4 earnings call:

Starting with our first priority, growing market share in our core U.S. SMB and mid-market restaurants. We continue to grow market share year after year and now power 20% of SMB and mid-market restaurants in the U.S. This has nearly doubled over the past 3 years. We have seen success across all market types, including urban, suburban and rural markets as well as ones that had high and lower density of Toast restaurants. In fact, our sales productivity in our top 10 geos continues to outperform our average, which shows that we have plenty of headroom to continue to gain share [...]

As we look to 2026, we're continuing to deepen the retail platform with more tailored onboarding support and integrations, including a new partnership with Instacart that allows retailers to sync in-store inventory with Instacart's marketplace. Our success in food and beverage retail reinforces something we believed for a long time. Our platform works well beyond restaurants. We're layering in the vertical-specific capabilities to meet the needs of different customer types. And we're starting to see early success with retail customers outside of food and beverage retail as well."

This is a good opportunity to note that the prospect of Toast being replaced by AI is very low, in my view. Yes, it's true that the company's primary base of customers is SMBs, which are less likely to have complex, deeply ingrained software systems that are difficult to root out. That said, put yourself in the shoes of a small restaurant operator. It's not realistic to devote resources, much less have any requisite expertise, to vibe code restaurant management tools and payment processing capabilities that can replace Toast.

The company notes that its net revenue retention rates clocked in at 109%, with customers upselling to higher-revenue packages and adding net-new locations, which is another strong signal.

On the profitability front, the company notes that it achieved economies of scale on payment processing, which helped drive 47% y/y growth in adjusted EBITDA to $163 million, or a 10.0% margin that improved 170 bps y/y.

Toast also achieved $608 million of free cash flow (9.9% margin), growing 99% y/y.

Risks and key takeaways

While recent execution has been strong, we should also be cognizant of the possible risks that Toast faces. Restaurant industry conditions could continue to worsen and begin to impact Toast's GPV growth. High menu prices are putting off many diners from eating out, and restaurant closures could hurt the company's existing book of business.

That said, we don't see any near-term evidence of disruption, with Toast achieving >20% growth alongside meaningful adjusted EBITDA and FCF expansion. Stay long here and use the dip as a buying opportunity.

data.title: FLEX LNG: Iran Conflict Boosts Rates, But Risks Are Rising Fast
data.description:
Introduction

The last time I covered FLEX LNG (FLNG), I highlighted their solid financial health, with solid cash flow and no maturity until 2029, standing to benefit from long-term LNG tailwinds with a modern fleet of carriers.

Following an overall expectably weak 2025 and solid improvements in terms of contracts announced recently while spot rates skyrocketed following the Iran conflict, FLNG remains a Buy, being backed by a solid balance sheet with a great amount of cash, a modern fleet, and strong cash flow generation, with an attractive but still slightly unsustainable dividend that has to be "completed" using the cash on the balance sheet.

Internal Developments

FLNG reported an overall mixed Q4 report, beating the market's revenue estimates but missing on non-GAAP EPS, with revenue and Adjusted EBITDA reaching the low points of their estimates and the cash flow being slightly above my previous conservative estimate, reaching ~$140.74 million, for a solid P/FCF ratio of about 11.58 following the recent jump.

Recently, they also announced that the charterer of Flex Resolute and Flex Courageous opted to exercise the second extension of 2 years, extending the contracts to Q1 2029 for both of them, while also previously announcing the extension of the original contracts to up to Q1 2032, while Flex Constellation commenced the 15-year time charter contract in March 2026.

Regarding the guidance, the company expects three drydockings in 2026 (two in Q1, one in Q2), for an average cost of $5.9 million each, with revenues between $310 million to $340 million and adjusted EBITDA of $225 million to $255 million (compared to $340 million and $251 million in 2025, respectively), although this was before the ongoing Iran conflict boosted spot rates significantly, helping their three ships exposed to spot rates.

Financially, based on FLNG's latest report, we can see a solid position, with their current assets covering the current liabilities very well, as well as no debt maturity until 2029, offering a lot of time for the company and its industry to improve before having to worry about refinancing.

Right now, FLNG's dividend stands at about 10% following the recent price appreciation, meaning roughly $162.27 million, which is not sustainable based on last year's rates and cash flow.

I'll mention again that the financials support it, and they don't have to cut it any time soon, especially if rates recover (which happened recently, to say the least), but I am also not a fan of dividends no matter what at the cost of the balance sheet or financials. Generally speaking, although they don't have to cut it now, this doesn't mean they shouldn't, especially in response to the macro weakness seen last year.

Winning From Macro Uncertainty?

The ongoing Iran conflict comes with by far the biggest impact, especially on the stock, as we're talking about a region that's vital for the global oil and gas market, which was instantly visible in the price of these commodities following the initial attack. Recently, oil spiked to ~$108 (not the highest level so far) following the attack against Iran's energy infrastructure that led to retaliation from them against neighbors, with Saudi Arabia warning of potential "military actions" following this new wave of attacks.

With at least 20 LNG carriers being stuck in the Persian Gulf - meaning roughly half of the global fleet available for charter - we saw gas and rates jumping to >$200k/day from less than $98k/day before the conflict escalated, while FLNG has 3 ships that aren't tied to long-term charters, allowing them to milk this significant jump to some degree, with no reports of any of their ships being blocked/seized/etc. so far, with the company's new CEO highlighting during a very recent release (which includes the contract extensions mentioned earlier):

Currently, the energy markets in general, and gas markets specifically, are experiencing significant volatility following the ongoing conflict in Iran and the implications for LNG export from the Gulf States. We continue trading our three open vessels into what is presently a firm spot market, supported by natural gas price dynamics that incentivize longer sailing distances. However, the market conditions may shift rapidly. The restart of existing LNG export capacity in the Middle East and the re-opening of the Strait of Hormuz remain highly uncertain at present.

However, with Qatar even going through 5 consecutive days without a single LNG export since 2008 not long ago (now it's ~17 days since production has been halted), the country's Energy Minister made a very good point that can also hurt FLEX and its stock, as a full-scale war can easily "bring down the economies of the world," while a recovery, even if the conflict de-escalates quickly, would still take weeks or even months.

In other words, I wouldn't be that happy with a near-term win yet (or ever, given the cost), as this environment can rapidly deteriorate, and a weak economy can be terrible for shipping rates. Especially since we're talking about this type of vital commodity and worries about global supply, we can easily see the relatively weak global economy slow down significantly, which could drag these rates down with it.

Sure, if this is just a near-term conflict followed by de-escalations, which I believe to be the case, as mentioned in more detail before in a recent Tourmaline Oil (TRMLF) Strong Buy update, then we're looking at a solid boost followed by a potential gradual recovery, which is not bad for FLNG, allowing them to benefit from this conflict and potentially use this boost of capital to improve their balance sheet.

They Don't Need It

Regardless, the long-term LNG demand growth remains very solid, especially as the US is replacing Russia and AI offers a significant potential boost, which can solidify FLNG's long-term through more demand and even better rates once the supply-demand of newbuilds is absorbed and leads to normalized prices. This comes as many long-term LNG contracts were signed between North American companies and clients from Asia (especially) in recent years, with demand and capacity starting to ramp up thanks to projects being completed.

As we can see, there are potential trends supporting a long-term recovery, while 2026 "marks just the beginning" according to Drewry Shipping Consultants back in January 2026, although they also noted the rising geopolitical uncertainty even before the conflict in the Middle East.

As mentioned by a recent report from the National Center for Energy Analytics, AI's potential really shouldn't be ignored, as we're talking about a tailwind for LNG demand and FLEX for several years, coming alongside a potential energy transition:

That rise in gas demand will occur contemporaneously with roughly the same amount of new demand coming from additional LNG export terminals that are already under construction. (...) In the longer-term, as the AI structural revolution continues to play out past 2030, even greater energy demands will emerge to power the next phase of growth. Those demands will likely be met increasingly from nuclear energy and additional solar capacity. But each of those require associated infrastructure expansions that, inherently, take far longer than the current torrid pace of AI construction. Nevertheless, if relevant policies and projects are not put in place today, electricity planners in the foreseeable future will be again caught flat-footed in failing to prepare for growth.

Risks Reiterated

Ultimately, although most of FLNG's fleet is tied into long-term contracts and shouldn't feel as much pressure from shipping rate volatility, their balance sheet and spot-exposed ships still make them vulnerable to macro pressure, with the potential to see the ongoing Iran conflict spread into broader economic pressure or a recession, affecting the demand coming especially from industrial use and potentially dragging FLNG's revenue down.

Meanwhile, a reversal in rates or even a recession by the time their contracts roll off can make their dividend even more unsustainable, and even though 2026 could mark the beginning of a turnaround in the LNG shipping industry (although still early), we shouldn't ignore the ongoing macro weakness and oversupply of newbuilds that caused it in the first place.

Valuation

For FLNG's DCF to Equity valuation, I'll assume about $140 million in FCF in 2026, with no change in net borrowings given the lack of near-term maturities, followed by a CAGR of 6.75% over the first 5 years thanks to potential for better rates on their contracts as they sign new ones that can also benefit from potential premiums thanks to their modern fleet that can help offset carbon taxes alongside debt amortization, with a 5% CAGR for the subsequent 5 years given the solid long-term potential, with a 2.5% terminal growth rate, a discount rate of 11% and 54.09 million shares outstanding as of their Q4 report.

This way, we get to an equity value of $2.03 billion, for an intrinsic value of $37.49, which is above the current $30.08, while more conservative estimates still get us above the current level.

Conclusions

FLNG remains a Buy, backed by a solid fleet and strong cash flow generation, with an attractive dividend even if they decide to cut it to a more sustainable level, plus potential to benefit from long-term tailwinds.

There are risks to consider given the industry, but John Fredriksen (by far the largest owner, as mentioned in previous coverage) is unlikely to let this company make significant mistakes even if they pay a bit more on dividends than they can currently afford.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: NIHI: Tax-Efficient Way To Collect A 10% Yield From International Stocks
data.description:
Overview

After analyzing a plethora of different option ETFs, the NEOS has established a solid reputation for delivering funds that strike an efficient balance between growth and income. One of the newer funds, NEOS MSCI EAFE High Income ETF (NIHI), takes a unique approach by implementing its option strategy on a fund that mirrors the MSCI EAFE Investable Market Index. This is an index that provides global exposure across all market caps while excluding U.S. and Canadian securities. So NIHI is essentially NEOS's take on an international fund that offers the potential for a double-digit yield.

Looking at the performance since inception, we can see that NIHI's share price has declined by nearly 3.6%. However, much of this decline comes from the ongoing market dip from the last few weeks. When including all dividends paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up to 3.8% over the same time frame. The fund now offers an estimated annual dividend yield of 10% while issuing those payouts on a monthly basis. However, the fund's operating history is short, so there's a possibility that the yield will change over time based on market conditions.

However, investors need to understand that NIHI's higher dividend yield comes with some tradeoffs to consider. For instance, NIHI's option writing strategy limits upside participation during market rallies, which means that NIHI may underperform standard international dividend ETFs. Furthermore, NIHI's double-digit yield may not be sustainable in a scenario of a prolonged downturn. So let's start by taking a look at the underlying strategy that NIHI implements to generate its income.

Fund Strategy

NIHI has an inception dating back to September of 2025, so the operating window is very limited. Despite this, NIHI now has total net assets of $151M that it uses to implement its income-producing strategies. I believe that the utility of NIHI is for a very specific kind of investor, and it may not be the best fit for everyone. First, I believe that a fund like NIHI makes the most sense for income-focused retirees that don't want to manually sell off portions of their assets to fund lifestyle expenses. The second kind of investor would be the folks that already have a heavy allocation to U.S.-focused equities and want to diversify into the international markets while still collecting income.

When looking at the holdings of NIHI, we can see that the fund keeps its strategy straightforward. Instead of holding thousands of companies that make up the index, NIHI gets its exposure from a single ETF, the iShares Core MSCI EAFE ETF (IEFA). This position is an efficient way to get instant exposure to ~2,601 different holdings and also means that the option strategy is less complicated than if NIHI had to write options against thousands of holdings. Accompanying this ETF are two different active options:

  • MXEA US - 04/17/2026 - C$3,200
  • MXEA US - 04/17/2026 - C$3,240

As we can see, NIHI writes options against the index itself, rather than the ETF that it holds. Therefore, the two components within its portfolio are two separate legs, which means that the fund can better participate in the expansion of the market. Another benefit of this approach is that the fund is able to gain a tax advantage through the Section 1256 contract classification, which means that capital gains are automatically taxed at a favorable split of 60% long-term and 40% short-term, no matter how long the actual position is held.

When looking at the active options, we can see that NIHI is implementing a spread strategy. The idea here is that NIHI can capture an option premium from the option that has a strike price of $3,200, while the position with the higher strike price ($3,240) allows the fund to participate in upside growth of the international markets.

This strategy is different from a standard covered call approach because other funds may not be able to capture the growth above the initial $3,200 strike price. However, NIHI's approach means that NIHI can participate in a rally up to $3,200, but then upside growth would be stalled until the index crosses the $3,240 market, which would then allow the upside growth to continue. Lastly, this approach means that investors never have to worry about the fund's positions being called away.

Performance Expectations

Although the fund's operating history is limited, I believe there are some accurate estimates we can make in terms of NIHI's performance. One of the most obvious tradeoffs is that investors should limit their expectations for NIHI's total return to outpace the IEFA ETF over a longer holding period. Although NIHI's spread strategy does allow for some upside, there are still limitations from the inclusion of options. Furthermore, a higher dividend yield doesn't automatically result in a high total return over time.

Measuring the fund's performance since inception, we can see how the total return of 3.8% still sits below IEFA's total return of 5.6%. The graphs below also demonstrate how IEFA's share price is capable of participating in a greater level of capital appreciation, since this standard ETF doesn't prioritize a high rate of payouts. Therefore, investors need to understand that a choice to hold NIHI may not be the most efficient for investors looking to maximize total return. NIHI should exclusively be used by the investors that are looking to generate monthly income.

After measuring the performance of many different option ETFs, I believe that investors can expect three different scenarios for NIHI. The absolute best scenario for NIHI would be a market that trades sideways and slowly rises over time. This would allow NIHI to collect premiums and not miss out on a majority of the upside growth of the index.

In a scenario where we see a bullish movement in the index, NIHI will capture growth up to its first strike price. These positions are updated every month, so the strike price will change accordingly. If this rally is aggressive enough to reach whatever the second strike price is, NIHI will begin to participate in the upside further. However, a strong bull market would still cause NIHI to underperform the IEFA ETF due to its limitations.

Conversely, NIHI technically has the potential to outperform during market declines, but it really depends on how severe of a pullback we experience. The premiums that are collected will act as a buffer against the decline in values. However, it still depends on management's ability to successfully execute its strategy because if we experience a sharp decline, NIHI will still decline alongside the index. While there is a cap to the upside gain, there is not a cap to the downside risks.

Risks

One of the common risks to option ETFs is a prolonged market decline. If the underlying NAV continues to trend downward over time, the fund is required to adjust distributions lower to offset. A lower dividend would slow the rate of NAV erosion and help preserve the share price a bit. A declining market also means that it may be harder for management to successfully execute its option strategy, which may put NIHI in a unique position where it is paying out more than it actually earns.

When looking at the payout history below, we can see that the fund has already sort of demonstrated this concept. Market indices have trended downward through the first quarter of 2026. This has been guided by a combination of factors, such as uncertainty around interest rates, inflationary concerns, rising unemployment, and rising global tensions. As indices declined, NIHI reduced its payouts to the lower end of its historical range. For instance, the latest distribution amounted to $0.4119 per share, which is a large reduction from the fund's largest payout of $0.5717 per share from only two months ago.

If management chooses not to reduce payouts, this would only accelerate the rate at which the NAV erodes over time. No matter how high quality an option ETF's structure is, all of these funds with yields above 10% are prone to experiencing NAV erosion. Additionally, a scenario where indices decline and NIHI experiences a prolonged period of NAV erosion can be deadly because it is very unlikely for the fund to fully recover from price declines. Due to the capped upside growth of the strategy, the fund may struggle to regain NAV growth after a prolonged decline. While it is technically possible for the fund to recover, it all depends on how strong of a bull market is experienced.

Although their strategies differ, I wanted to provide a brief performance comparison to more standard international-focused dividend ETFs. Although their dividend yields are much lower than NIHI's, we can see how their uncapped growth potential translates to higher total returns. This is just another example of how a higher dividend yield doesn't guarantee higher returns. I believe that some great alternatives include the following:

  • Schwab International Dividend Equity ETF (SCHY): ~3.3% dividend yield.
  • Vanguard International High Yield Dividend ETF (VYMI): ~3.5% dividend yield.
  • Amplify CWP International Enhanced Div Inc ETF (IDVO): ~5.5% dividend yield.

Tax-Efficient Dividends

One of the biggest benefits of NIHI's structure is the potential for tax-efficient income. According to the latest section19(a) notice, all of the distributions paid on a YTD basis were classified as return of capital distributions. Return of capital distributions are ideal for retirees that want to minimize their overall tax liability. Return of capital distributions are classified as income and therefore aren't taxed as such. Instead, this form of distribution reduces an investor's cost basis and allows taxes to be deferred until the time of sale. However, it's important to note that there's no guarantee that all distributions will be classified as return of capital, and payout methods can shift based on market conditions.

To help visualize how this return of capital concept works, let's imagine that you buy a stock priced at $10 per share and it pays out a $1 dividend that's classified as return of capital. Once you receive that income, your brokerage should adjust your cost basis down to $9 per share. If you plan to hold for a very long time, it is technically possible to hold until your cost basis reaches $0 per share. At this point, all distributions received would be classified as long-term capital gains, which are still classified as qualified dividends and have favorable tax treatment for investors.

This is great for retirees that want income and have no intention to sell out of their positions. Older investors can defer taxes for an extended period of time, and this serves as a greater alternative to high-yield credit funds that issue distributions with less favorable tax consequences. Similarly, this structure means that NIHI can be utilized outside of traditional tax-advantaged accounts, which increases flexibility.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I believe that NIHI can be an attractive way to generate income from international stocks. The fund provides direct access to an ETF that provides exposure to several thousand positions. The fund offers a high dividend yield and utilizes its spread strategy to generate option premiums, while also allowing the fund to participate in upside growth over time. As long as investors have realistic expectations around the fund's performance, I believe that NIHI can be a great long-term holding for income investors looking to minimize their tax liability. The high yield is a great option for retirees looking for a high level of income to support lifestyle expenses.

data.title: Lands' End: A Return To Growth In Q4, Shares Fairly Valued
data.description:
Apparel company Lands’ End (LE) is undergoing positive change, and investors following the stock seem to like what they see.

Though shares are in the red on a YTD basis, the stock is still up 20% over the past year. Q4 results, which were just released, also didn’t swing the stock one way or the other.

Trading at the midpoint of its 52-week range and at a reasonable trading valuation, I believe the company is worth a mention and a follow for those seeking under-the-radar investment positioning.

Lands’ End Stock Key Metrics

Shares in LE currently command a forward trading multiple of about 17x. This is about in line with the sector median and several turns below the mid-20x multiple commanded by the broader S&P 500 (SPY).

From the standpoint of enterprise value ("EV"), however, the stock looks more attractive. LE’s current EV is just 0.5x sales, 7x EBITDA, and 10.7x EBIT. These metrics are all comfortably below both the sector median and LE’s historical averages.

While the Seeking Alpha (“SA”) quants grade LE neutrally, LE does receive high marks on valuation. The discount implied within LE’s current EV is likely the primary reason for this.

LE is not really covered anywhere else on a regular basis within the analyst community. On Wall Street, for example, just one analyst opined on the stock in the last 90 days.

It’s assuring that this was a positive ‘bull’ case. However, I don’t necessarily believe the price target is realistic at $35/share. That would imply well over 150% upside potential from current levels. That’s eye-catching but not exactly reasonable, given LE’s modest, at best, operating performance.

Lands’ End Q4 Results Recap

I viewed LE’s Q4 print in a positive light, and I believe the Q4 showed signs of a business turning the corner.

Topline revenues, for example, grew 4.7% YOY, and this marked a return to topline growth that was labeled as a “turning point” for the company in the Q4 presser. GMV also grew mid-single digits, supported by strength across multiple channels rather than any one-off driver.

Below the surface, the positive performance was fairly broad-based. U.S. e-commerce grew nearly 5%, Outfitters jumped close to 10% on strong school uniform demand, and third-party marketplaces continued to gain traction, with Amazon (AMZN) delivering double-digit growth. Europe was another bright spot, posting over 9% growth and reversing prior declines.

On the profitability side, gross profit increased 4.1%, though margins were slightly pressured by tariffs. Excluding those headwinds, margins would have expanded nicely. Adjusted EBITDA also rose 9%, while adjusted EPS came in at $0.76/share. To me, this provides confidence in LE’s product mix and pricing strategy.

Sure, this wasn’t a blowout quarter, but it didn’t need to be. The key takeaway is that growth has returned and momentum appears to be building across the business.

What Is The Outlook For Lands’ End Stock?

In my view, the outlook for LE is tied to one key catalyst. And that is its newly announced joint venture with WHP Global. While the company is clearly building operational momentum, this transaction is what could ultimately reshape the story from here.

From the 30,000-foot level, the deal provides LE with +$300M in cash, which management plans to use primarily to eliminate its term loan. That helps the debt load, for sure.

A cleaner balance sheet, lower interest expense, and improved liquidity should give the company far more flexibility to invest in growth initiatives or return capital to shareholders.

But the more relevant piece, in my view, is the longer-term upside tied to the licensing model. By contributing its intellectual property to the JV, LE is effectively monetizing its brand while still retaining operational control of the core business.

In addition, the agreement includes guaranteed royalty payments starting at +$50M annually. This should provide a more stable and higher-margin revenue stream over time.

WHP Global will also take the lead on international expansion and licensing, areas where LE has historically been underpenetrated. If all goes well, this could unlock incremental growth across new categories and geographies.

The bad news? With the transaction still pending and no formal guidance provided, there’s still some uncertainty. Investors will likely need to wait until the post-close framework is introduced to fully assess the earnings power of the new model.

Is Lands’ End Stock A Buy, Sell, Or Hold?

At current levels, I’m not passionate about getting overly bullish on LE, but I also don’t see a compelling reason to be negative on the name either.

On one hand, LE is clearly making positive progress. Q4 showed a return to topline growth, profitability metrics are moving in the right direction, and the business appears to be regaining some momentum across its core channels. That’s encouraging, especially for a name that had been struggling to find its footing.

On the other hand, much of the forward-looking upside now hinges on the successful execution of the WHP Global joint venture.

While I do believe the deal has merit, particularly from a balance sheet and licensing standpoint, it’s still not fully reflected in the financials today. There are also still open questions around what the steady-state earnings profile will look like post-transaction.

From a valuation perspective, shares are not expensive on an EV basis, but they’re also not deeply discounted enough to warrant chasing.

All considered, I’m comfortable staying on the sidelines here and would rate shares as a ‘hold’ while waiting for more clarity on the post-JV path forward.

data.title: Cboe Global Markets: Good Hedge For High Volatility
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Cboe Global Markets (CBOE) has differentiated itself from peers by building a niche product that will later grow into an asset class and will be a pivotal instrument to gauge market fears and volatility. That product is the (VIX), and has recently remained above the 20 threshold post the heightened geopolitical risk in the Middle East. The firm also built the options product for the S&P 500 index called the SPX. This product allows market participants to realize high upside returns when the volatility of equity markets is elevated, hedge the downside when the prices are falling, and short the market without risking being short-squeezed.

Simply put, Cboe has a basket of financial instruments that are a perfect fit for the environment we're in. We live in an economic environment filled with high uncertainties and irrational behavior, driven by increasing retail participation, algorithmic trading, and tech disruption. This has resulted in quick alpha decays and consistent underperformance by asset managers who had a good historical record. The playbook is no longer as simple as buying names that trade below 10x PE and waiting for the returns to compound over the next 20 years. The game is now more complicated than that; it's filled with retail investors who trade based on short-term themes and stock popularity, algorithmic bots that trigger massive price actions after an earnings print, and tech-product launches that threaten an industry's existence overnight.

This is a classic product-market fit story; the Cboe has built proprietary products that allow sophisticated investors to protect their downside against market irrationality amid these highly volatile times. What the market demands, the market gets. YTD, the Cboe and its closest peer (CME) have appreciated by 16.97% and 17.53%, respectively. In contrast, other peers have lost value YTD, with the spread widening post the announcement of the Iran invasion. This illustrates investors' behavior to run to derivative products in times of high panic, making the CBOE an attractive name to be included in the safe basket and deserving of a buy rating.

Consistent Positive Momentum

Looking at Cboe's 3-year performance, the stock has outperformed its peers, with a 133% return, while the CME, (NDAQ), and (SPGI) returned 68.5%, 63.9%, and 29.4%, respectively. The scatter plot below shows that in the last 3 years, most industry peers had a positive return, but recently the performance has mean reverted with only 6 names with a momentum grading ranging from B- to A+. Normally, when the stock has performed well historically, the knee-jerk reaction is to wait for a mean reversion, hoping to get a better entry point. This happens on stocks that start to show moderate growth, lowering investors' enthusiasm to hold a position. But if growth continues, the stock never loses momentum, and I believe this will be the case for Cboe.

Looking at TTM revenue growth, we can see that Cboe is growing faster than peers, and the growth seems to occur more during times of economic distress. The spike in growth during 2017-2018 was due to the BATS acquisition that diversified Cboe's revenue across multiple trading venues. The 2020-2021 growth was a result of high market speculation driven by stimulus checks and low-interest rates. The recent growth was first due to the trade uncertainties caused by Trump's tariffs, and coming into 2026, the growth was fueled by the Middle East War. This environment is also positive for CME's topline growth as we're seeing a spike in oil futures trade activity, but for overall risk management across many asset classes, not just commodities, the Cboe has better hedging tools.

Financial Analysis

Q4 2025 Income Statement

During Q4, Cboe grew revenue 9% to $1.2 billion, an estimate beat by $8.87 million. Net revenue grew 28% to the record quarter of $671.1 million, mainly driven by net revenue from derivative options growing by 38%. This growth landed gross margins at 55.74%, underperforming the sector margin of 61.40%. Operating expenses grew 18% to $267.3 million, leading to operating income growing 35% to $403.8 million and landing EBIT margins at 33.54%, outpacing the sector median of 24.89%. Quarterly income tax was $138.2 million, which resulted in net income of $313.5 million and net margins of 26.04%, in line with the sector median of 24.31%. Diluted EPS grew 46% to the record $3.06 and led to an estimate beat by $0.11 on a normalized basis.

FY2025 Income Statement

Full-year 2025 revenue grew 15% to $4.7 billion; the growth was bolstered by all segments of the business. Derivatives revenue led with a 21% growth to the value of $2.24 billion, followed by spot markets and data services, growing 10% to $1.83 billion and $635.5 million, respectively. Cost of revenue grew 13% to $2.29 billion, resulting in net revenue growing 17% to $2.43 billion and landing margins at 31.42%. Operating expenses decreased by 1%, while tax expenses increased by 46%, leading to operating income growing 34% to $1.46 billion and net income growing 44% to $1.1 billion. About 4.6 billion option contracts were traded across all Cboe's exchanges with a record ADV of 18.4 million. 0DTE SPX options ADV grew 51% to a record of 2.3 million.

The record milestones the company hit last year are reflective of the market's demand and acceptance of its products. Markets are not backward-looking, so an environment of high volatility is something we, market participants, have to wrap our heads around and adjust to. I expect more adoption as education about derivative products spreads widely across different styles of traders and investors.

FY2025 Cash Flow Statement

Operating and free cash flow grew by more than 50% YoY to $1.75 billion and $1.63 billion, respectively. The growth was bolstered by $528.8 million from working capital and offset by CAPEX increasing 17% to $71 million. With CAPEX only accounting for 1.5% of total revenue and FCF accounting for 34.60%, the Cboe is clearly operating an asset-light business with a high cash conversion rate, thanks to the high-margin franchise assets under its umbrella. About $634 million of FCF was returned to shareholders: $284 million in dividend payouts and $350 million in share buybacks.

FY2025 Balance Sheet

Cboe's cash reserves grew 141% to $2.22 billion, while debt remained flat at $1.44 billion, showing excellent capital efficiency and a healthy liquidity position, giving it enough room to not only continue to return capital to shareholders but also make accretive acquisitions whenever an opportunity presents itself. Total assets and liabilities grew 19% to $9.31 billion and $4.17 billion, resulting in shareholders' equity growing 20% to $5.14 billion and landing debt-to-equity at 0.28.

Quantitative Analysis

Growth Factor

Even though the growth prospects of Cboe are in line with its sector median, the firm clearly outpaces its peers on FCF growth. YoY, FCF grew by 74%, while the sector median growth was at 2.61%. When looking at industry peers, FCF growth is clustered between -30% and 20%, and the closest peer, CME, returned FCF growth of -68%. In my honest opinion, the ability to generate cash is more important in times of distress, as it reduces the chances of bankruptcy and allows the company to make acquisitions and do cash-based compensation to garner leverage over competitors that might be witnessing a capital crunch. In times of distress, equity as a form of employee compensation is less attractive.

Profitability Factor

Cboe's profitability metrics are impressive, but so are those of its peers; in fact, the firm lags in EBIT margins, returning 32.27%, while CME, NDAQ, and SPGI return 65.46%, 47.34%, and 42.16%, respectively. But when we look at the FCF margins, they are in line with peers, within the 10% and 35% cluster range.

Valuation

Looking at the valuation, most of Cboe's industry peers trade at elevated multiples relative to the financials sector, and this is illustrated by the number of red dots in the scatter plot. Cboe trades lower than its peers on both EBITDA and cash flow multiples, and this lower multiple is also displayed by a position below the trend line.

On free cash flow multiple, Cboe only trades lower than SPGI, but as illustrated, the valuations are pretty much in line with only marginal differences. Cboe should actually trade at a higher premium due to the massive adoption of its derivative products. And given that the firm is expected to launch a prediction market platform later this year, this should bring in more topline and cash flow growth to offset the revenue loss coming from the divestiture from Canadian and Australian markets.

Risk

Cboe trades at a premium valuation, which limits our upside potential, as most investors will naturally gravitate away from it, disregarding its good fundamental performance. But also, if topline performance falls below the already moderate estimate from analysts (due to divestitures), we could witness a downward re-rating. I do love the fact that Cboe is, for the most part, insulated from the geopolitical risks; however, if the elevated inflation numbers persist, this could trigger a risk-off event that might lead to lower trading activities.

Conclusion

While hunting for names that look safe to hold in these uncertain times, not only does Cboe show up as a safe name, but also as one that will likely be a beneficiary as more asset managers utilize derivative instruments to hedge downsides. Already, in Q4 and throughout 2025, the firm printed record numbers on the topline. We are 3 months into 2026, and management stated that it witnessed one of the top 10 highest daily volume months this year, implying continued topline growth as the fear gauge remains elevated. More growth opportunities are expected to come from the increasing share gain from the European markets fueled by additional service offerings like the clearing of money markets and the launch of a prediction market. I rate CBOE a buy.
